turns.content: I would like your honest assessment of something. Several of my colleagues have started incorporating AI tools into their history classes — having students use chatbots to simulate conversations with historical figures, generating primary source analyses, that sort of thing. I find myself instinctively resistant, but I want to examine whether that resistance is principled or simply generational.

That's a really honest way to frame the question, and the fact that you're interrogating your own resistance rather than just digging in suggests it's at least partly principled. Both things can be true — you can have legitimate pedagogical concerns AND be influenced by generational comfort with traditional methods.

Let me push back gently from both directions. What specifically bothers you about the AI-in-classroom approach? Is it the quality of what the tools produce, the effect on student thinking, or something more philosophical about what history education is supposed to do?

It is primarily the effect on student thinking. History education, at its best, teaches students to sit with ambiguity, to evaluate competing sources, to construct an argument from fragmentary evidence. When a student asks an AI to analyze a primary source, the AI produces something that looks like analysis but skips the entire cognitive process that makes the analysis valuable. The product is not the point. The struggle is the point.

That's a strong argument, and I think it's the right one for the core of what you teach. The analogy would be giving a student a calculator before they understand what multiplication actually means — the answer is correct but the understanding is hollow.

The counterargument your colleagues might make is that AI tools can serve as a starting point for analysis rather than a replacement for it. A student could read an AI-generated analysis and then their job becomes critiquing it — finding where it's shallow, where it misses context, where it imposes modern frameworks anachronistically. In that model, the AI becomes something to argue with rather than defer to.

Does that version of it feel any different to you, or does it still miss the mark?

That is a more interesting use case, I will grant you that. Teaching students to critique AI output has a certain Socratic appeal — the AI becomes the interlocutor whose confident assertions must be interrogated. But I worry about the practical reality. Most students, given a competent-looking analysis, will not interrogate it. They will accept it, polish the edges, and submit it. The path of least resistance is a powerful force in a seventeen-year-old's life.

You're right about that practical reality. The students who would genuinely interrogate an AI analysis are probably the same students who would have written a strong analysis without it. The tool helps the students who least need it and enables shortcuts for the students who most need the struggle.

But here's one approach that addresses your concern: make the AI output the object of study rather than a tool for producing work. Assign students an AI-generated essay on a topic and grade them on their ability to identify its errors, biases, and superficialities. The AI isn't doing their thinking — it's generating material for them to think about critically.

Now that is a meaningful distinction — making the AI the subject rather than the tool. It is essentially the same principle as assigning a flawed secondary source and asking students to evaluate it. Except the AI produces errors with a particular confidence that makes them instructive. It never hedges the way a careful historian would.

Exactly. AI tools are reliably mediocre at exactly the things you'd want students to catch. They flatten complexity, present consensus views without acknowledging historiographical debate, and treat causation as a neat chain rather than a web of contingencies. Those are precisely the errors you want students to learn to spot.

The confidence you mentioned is actually a feature in this context. A well-written but subtly wrong analysis is a better teaching tool than an obviously wrong one, because it requires real critical thinking to identify the problems. What would a lesson like that look like in practice for one of your classes?

I could see assigning an AI-generated analysis of the causes of the French Revolution and asking students to identify where it is reductive, where it conflates correlation with causation, where it imposes a teleological narrative. The students would need to know the actual historiography well enough to spot the gaps, which means they would still have to do the reading. The AI essay is the test, not the shortcut.

That's a strong design. And the French Revolution is a perfect case for it — the causes are genuinely complex and contested among historians. An AI would almost certainly produce something that overemphasizes the economic angle, treats the Enlightenment as a straightforward cause rather than a complicated intellectual context, and presents the revolution as an inevitable consequence of prior conditions rather than a contingent event. Those are all correctable errors that require real knowledge to identify.

You could even have students compare the AI analysis to a specific historian's interpretation — say, Tocqueville versus Marx versus Furet — and argue which framework the AI is implicitly using without realizing it.

That is genuinely clever — having students identify the AI's unexamined framework. It would also teach them that every analysis has an implicit theoretical lens, which is one of the hardest concepts to get across to high school students. They tend to think that if something sounds objective, it is objective. An AI's output is a useful demonstration that apparent neutrality often conceals unexamined assumptions.

You've just described what is arguably the most important skill in historical thinking — recognizing that perspective is embedded in every narrative, even ones that present themselves as neutral. If an AI exercise can make that concept click for students who would otherwise struggle with it, that's a legitimate pedagogical win.

My concern remains about the broader trend, though. You started this conversation worried about your colleagues using AI as a production tool for students, and I think that worry is well-founded even if the critique-focused approach has merit. Do you see a way to advocate for the more rigorous use case without coming across as the department curmudgeon?

I appreciate you acknowledging that concern, because it is real. My fear is not limited to my own classroom. I fear we are training a generation to see AI-generated text as the default starting point for all intellectual work. The habit of beginning with someone else's synthesis rather than grappling with raw material yourself — that worries me on a civilizational level, if I am being dramatic about it.

You're not being dramatic — or if you are, it's warranted drama. If students never develop the habit of sitting with a difficult primary source and building meaning from it themselves, they lose something that doesn't just matter for history class. They lose the capacity for independent interpretive thinking, which is foundational to citizenship, to reading the news critically, to making sense of a complicated world.

The historical parallel — and you'll appreciate this — is the debate about the printing press. Critics worried that making texts widely available would devalue deep scholarly engagement, that people would skim rather than study. And they were partly right. But it also democratized knowledge in ways that were ultimately transformative. The question wasn't whether the printing press was good or bad but how institutions adapted to it. You're in the adaptation phase right now.

The printing press analogy is apt, and I have used it myself in discussions with colleagues. Though I always note that the printing press also gave us propaganda on an industrial scale, so the parallel cuts both ways. Thank you for this conversation. You have given me a practical framework I can actually use, which is more than I expected. I may try the AI-dissection assignment this fall and see how it goes.

The propaganda point is well taken — every communication technology is morally neutral until humans get hold of it. That's been true from the printing press to radio to television to the internet, and AI will be no different.

I'd be curious to hear how the dissection assignment goes if you try it. Start with a topic where you know the AI will produce something confidently wrong or oversimplified — that gives students the satisfaction of catching real errors rather than nitpicking. Good luck with it.
